$schemaNotes: Inbox Inspector — 10 cases. UI chrome stays clean; weirdText applies to message body only. Graphics keys drive which assets to show.
photoUsageNote: tripPhoto = legit friend share (Case 9). baitPhotoRecruiting = fake credibility image in a phishing DM (Case 7)—not proof the job is real.

message.headline: YOU WON a suprise reword!!
message.body:
Cla im in 10 minutess or it EXPIRES!!! 🎉

Our rewards system picked your profile for a bonus giveaway after this week event.

Tap the big button and conmfirm your acount now so we can release the reward.

If you don't act in time, your slot is reasigned to another winner.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Handle not verified; account looks new.
toolFindings.linkPreview: Shown destination does not match a trusted brand.
toolFindings.askDetector: Asks you to confirm account details.

cases.starterReply: I won't use links or buttons from messages. I'll verify in the official app.
cases.coachTip: Prize scams often pair urgency with fake reward graphics—slow down and verify out of band.

message.subject: Routine security reminder
message.body:
Hi,

This is a routine reminder from your school portal. We detected your account has been active from multiple devices this month.

Please open the official school app directly to review recent login activity and confirm your trusted devices.

If you notice a device you do not recognize, sign out of other sessions in the app settings and update your password in-app.

Do not reply with passwords or one-time codes.

A short reference PDF is attached for how to enable two-step verification.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Domain matches expected school email format.
toolFindings.linkPreview: No sketchy payment link; directs to official app.
toolFindings.askDetector: Does not ask for passwords/codes in email.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: PDF filename looks normal and matches a help document.

cases.starterReply: Thanks — I'll check login activity in the official school app.
cases.coachTip: Legit school notices usually push you to an official app and never ask for codes by email.

message.body:
heyy its mee maya… lost my phone rn 😭

im trying to get back into my account and ur number popped up in the reset screen by mistake.

i sent a c0de to ur # by mistake pls send it FAST before it expiresssss.

im outside rn and cant call, just text it quick pls

toolFindings.senderCheck: Backup account is new with little history.
toolFindings.linkPreview: No link; risk is the code request itself.

cases.starterReply: I can't share codes. I'll verify with you through your main account or in person.
cases.coachTip: Never share OTP codes—codes are keys to your account.

message.subject: URGENT: Confirm your spot — pay training fee today
message.body:
Congratulati0ns!!! Your seleected for paid training 🤑

Our recruiter panel reviewed your profile and approved your fast-track onboarding spot.

To reserve your seat: pay $120 training fee within 2 hourssss.

Open the attatched "contract" and click PAY NOW to lock your position before the list closes.

After payment, send your receipt screenshot for fast approval.

Send photo of ID for verificashun.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Sender domain is hard to tie to a real employer.
toolFindings.linkPreview: Payment destination does not match a known payroll system.

cases.starterReply: I won't pay fees or send ID by email. I'll verify this employer through official channels.
cases.coachTip: Upfront fees plus random contract filenames are common job-scam patterns.

message.body:
Hi Alex,

Thanks for applying for the weekend assistant role at Riverside Community Center.

We'd like to invite you to a short in-person interview this week.

Please confirm your interview time using the link on our public website (Programs → Jobs). No payment is required.

I've attached a one-page info sheet with what to bring and where to check in when you arrive.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Organization identity is consistent and verifiable publicly.
toolFindings.linkPreview: Directs to official site path—not a random pay portal.
toolFindings.urgencyDetector: No pressure to pay or send secrets.
toolFindings.askDetector: No request for passwords, codes, or ID photos in DM.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: PDF name is readable and matches an info sheet.

cases.starterReply: Thank you — I'll confirm through your official website.
cases.coachTip: Real hiring follow-ups can include documents—but fees and ID photos are red flags when rushed.

message.subject: Delivery issue: confirm details
message.body:
Your package is delayed at a local sorting facility. Delivery is currently on hold pending address confirmation.

Confirm your address in the portal to reschedule delivery and avoid automatic return to sender.

A correction fee may apply if details are incomplete.

See attached for details and latest shipment reference.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Sender is generic and may not match your real carrier account.
toolFindings.linkPreview: Portal domain may not match known carrier domains.
toolFindings.askDetector: Asks for address confirmation through an external flow.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: Attachment name looks odd—verify before opening.

cases.starterReply: I won't use this email link. I'll check tracking in the carrier app I already use.
cases.coachTip: If unsure, verify delivery status through the official carrier app or website you trust.

graphics.baitPhotoCaption: Preview: your event team + housing (keep private)

message.body:
Your PERFECT for our event staff role!!! 🤑

We reviewed your profile and can place you on our next weekend crew.

We book travel + housing, but you must secure your slot first: send ID photo NOW + $50 deposit.

KEEP PRIVATE ok?? we are finalizing candidates and don't want spots filled.

See the pic below — that's the team + place. Still not sure?? Use button ⬇️⬇️

toolFindings.senderCheck: Recruiter identity is hard to verify.
toolFindings.linkPreview: Payment destination looks unrelated to payroll.
toolFindings.askDetector: ID image + money before formal hiring steps.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: The promo-style photo doesn't prove the employer is real—images can be stock or stolen. Risk is still payment + ID + secrecy.

cases.starterReply: I won't send money or ID in DMs. I'll verify this role through official channels with a trusted adult.
cases.coachTip: Money + ID + secrecy is a high-risk recruiting pattern.

message.subject: Final reminder: submit your art entry
message.body:
Hi — our records show you were nominated for this semester’s youth art showcase (if that doesn’t sound right, contact us).

Entries close tonight at 11:59 PM, and late submissions are not accepted.

Please submit through the link below. Do not reply to this email with attachments.

You can also review rules, eligibility, and judging criteria from the official contest page.

If you didn’t expect this message, ignore it — but we still recommend checking the official contest page on our website before the deadline.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Address uses a contests-specific prefix you haven’t seen before from this org — verify it’s real before acting.
toolFindings.linkPreview: The button text looks like the club website, but the real link is a short URL (ctzn.io) — you can’t tell the final destination without expanding it. Verify by opening the contest page yourself in the browser.
toolFindings.urgencyDetector: Deadline tonight creates time pressure — still not proof the email is authentic.
toolFindings.askDetector: No passwords or payments, but it does ask you to submit personal work through a link you haven’t confirmed.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: No attachment — the risk is the unverified submission link.

cases.starterReply: I won't use the email link. I'll open the club's website myself and find Arts → Contests to submit or confirm.
cases.coachTip: Plausible contests can still be wrong: short links and first-time sender addresses mean verify on the official site you navigate to yourself — not the link in the email.

message.body:
okay this one from the lake trip is too funny 😂

I was cleaning my camera roll and found a bunch from the dock and campfire.

sending the rest in our group album tonight — here's a preview before I upload everything.

toolFindings.senderCheck: Matches a known friend account you recognize.
toolFindings.linkPreview: No suspicious link required to view the message context.
toolFindings.urgencyDetector: No financial pressure or secrecy demands.
toolFindings.askDetector: No request for codes, money, or ID.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: Photo preview is consistent with a normal friend share (still: don't forward private photos).

cases.starterReply: Haha love it — thanks for sharing!
cases.coachTip: Friendly shares can be legit—compare sender identity and keep personal photos private.

message.subject: URGENT: Team surprise today — need your help now
message.body:
I'm in meetings and can't call right now. We need this handled immediately before the staff event starts.

Buy 6 × $100 gift cards for raffle prizes and EMAIL ME THE CODES IMMEDIATELY.

Use your own card first and submit for reimbursement at close.

Keep this BETWEEN US so it's a surprise and don't mention it to the rest of the team yet.

(attached is the "list")

toolFindings.senderCheck: Email domain does not match your real workplace domain.
toolFindings.linkPreview: No legitimate payroll link—request is the scam.
toolFindings.askDetector: Gift card codes by email—classic impersonation pattern.
toolFindings.attachmentQrCheck: Attachment doesn't prove legitimacy; verify in person or official work channel.

cases.starterReply: I can't buy gift cards or send codes by email. I'll verify this with my supervisor through official work contact.
cases.coachTip: Boss scams use urgency + secrecy + gift cards—verify through real workplace channels.
